rationale: This is a canonical example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, featuring the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich phenol (resorcinol) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (zinc chloride) and a Brønsted acid (HCl), leading to the formation of an aryl ketone (2,4-dihydroxyacetophenone) after hydrolysis of the intermediate ketimine salt. The reactants and product are simple, acyclic where possible, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of this named reaction.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a simple, highly activated phenol (resorcinol) and a basic nitrile (acetonitrile) to avoid ambiguity with other acylation reactions like Friedel-Crafts. The use of a nitrile as the acylating agent is the defining characteristic of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, distinguishing it from reactions that use acyl halides or anhydrides. The simple structures minimize potential overlap with more complex, polycyclic aromatic chemistry or other named reactions involving phenols.
